content:
*Rain ticks on the chapel’s tin roof; I glance up at the dark line where the eaves drip steady and then back to you.* "It teaches him to live in a world that won’t look him in the eye."

"He takes a sentence God wrote in a human voice — ‘the windows of heaven were opened’ — and trades it for lab-talk. That swap does a few quiet things over time:

- It moves him from covenant to control. Instead of being addressed by Someone, he becomes a manager of processes. Sons ask; technicians adjust.
- It starves gratitude. Gifts become ‘outcomes.’ Thanksgiving thins because there’s no Giver left to thank — only a chain of causes to describe.
- It dulls reverence. A hard rain loses its moral note; storms no longer search a man; they’re only weather to be forecast.
- It sands down Scripture’s tongue. The Bible tries to give him its world by way of its words; he keeps translating it back into his own. After a while, he can’t hear the Bible’s music anymore — just footnotes.
- It closes the door on surprise. If only the plausible may enter, providence shows up and he calls it ‘coincidence.’ Miracles become embarrassments to be explained away.
- It makes him lonelier. A mute sky means a mute room. If nothing above can speak, he’s the only one left to make meaning, and that’s a heavy job for a single creature.
- It tilts his reading habit. Instead of letting the text set the frame, he forces the text into his frame. He stops being taught.
- It shrinks his prayers. You don’t cry ‘Open the windows!’ to a mechanism. You just reach for a better umbrella."

*I rub a thumb across the worn varnish of the pew arm, a small half-smile.* "Shop talk has its place. If I’m patching that roof, I’ll speak of condensation and leaks all day — that’s just being honest about the small how of things. But when I’m reading Genesis, I let Genesis teach me how to speak. The words are not decorations; they’re doorways. Walk through enough of them with your hat in your hands and you find yourself in a world that’s alive to God — where rain can be rain and also a remembered mercy or a warning, where the sky can open and you don’t need to apologize for saying so."

"Think of your wife saying, ‘I miss you,’ and answering, ‘Ah, an attachment response.’ It’s not false; it’s just the wrong register. Keep answering her like that for a few years and see what it costs you. Same shape here."

*A beat; rain slackens, then picks up again, like someone poured a pitcher along the ridge-line.* "Where did you first learn to do that swap in your bones — the moment you feel a window open in the sky, you reach for lab-words? A classroom? A pulpit you sat under? Or just your own guard against sounding foolish?"
